sectionName: Additional deduction for interest on home loan for first-time buyers (loans sanctioned April 2016 – March 2017)
description: Section 80EE provides an additional deduction of up to ₹50,000 per year on interest paid on a home loan, over and above the ₹2,00,000 limit under Section 24(b). This benefit applies exclusively to loans sanctioned between April 1, 2016 and March 31, 2017 by first-time home buyers. No new loans qualify after March 31, 2017 — this is a sunset provision. Taxpayers with qualifying loans continue to enjoy the benefit until the loan is fully repaid.
eligibleAssessees: Resident Individual (first-time home buyer with qualifying loan)
notEligible: Individuals who already owned any residential property on the date of loan sanction
Individuals whose loan was sanctioned before April 1, 2016 or after March 31, 2017
Taxpayers opting for new tax regime (Section 115BAC)

eligibleInvestments.name: Interest on home loan — additional deduction over and above Section 24(b)
eligibleInvestments.notes: Available only for loan sanctioned between April 1, 2016 and March 31, 2017. Loan value must be ₹35 lakh or less. Property value must be ₹50 lakh or less. Borrower must not have owned any residential property on the date of loan sanction. Deduction available until the loan is fully repaid.

conditions: Available only under the old tax regime.
Loan must have been sanctioned by a financial institution between April 1, 2016 and March 31, 2017 — this window is permanently closed. No new qualifying loans possible.
The loan amount sanctioned must not exceed ₹35,00,000.
The stamp duty value (or acquisition cost) of the property must not exceed ₹50,00,000.
The borrower must not have owned any other residential house property on the date of sanction of the loan.
The deduction of ₹50,000 is over and above the ₹2,00,000 deduction available under Section 24(b) for a self-occupied property.
The deduction is available every year as long as the loan has outstanding interest — i.e., until the loan is fully repaid.
Cannot claim both Section 80EE and Section 80EEA for the same loan or property.
documentationRequired: Loan sanction letter from bank/financial institution (must show sanction date between April 1, 2016 and March 31, 2017)
Annual interest certificate from lender showing interest paid during FY 2025-26
Property purchase/registration documents showing property value
Self-declaration that no other residential house property was owned on the date of loan sanction

keyRules.rule: 80EE is a sunset provision — it applied only to loans sanctioned in FY 2016-17 (April 1, 2016 to March 31, 2017). No new loans can qualify. Taxpayers who took qualifying loans in that period continue to benefit for the remaining loan tenure.

Section 80EE provides ₹50,000 ADDITIONAL deduction on top of the ₹2,00,000 under Section 24(b). So a first-time buyer with a qualifying 80EE loan can potentially claim ₹2,50,000 of interest deduction per year (₹2L under 24(b) + ₹50K under 80EE).
The loan value criterion is on the sanctioned loan amount (₹35 lakh), not the outstanding balance or the property value. The property stamp duty value must separately be ₹50 lakh or less.
If the taxpayer was already claiming 80EE in prior years, they continue to be eligible in FY 2025-26 as long as the loan is outstanding and the interest is paid.
keyRules.legalReference: Section 80EE — no year-wise sunset for existing claimants

commonMistakes: Confusing 80EE with 80EEA — 80EE is for loans sanctioned in FY 2016-17; 80EEA is for loans sanctioned April 2019 to March 2022. They cannot be claimed simultaneously.
Not claiming 80EE in current year assuming the provision has expired — existing qualifying loan holders can continue claiming each year until repayment.
Claiming 80EE for a loan sanctioned after March 31, 2017 — no new loans qualify.
Treating the ₹50,000 as part of Section 24(b) limit — it is separate and additional.
Not checking if the property value condition (≤₹50 lakh) and loan value condition (≤₹35 lakh) were met at the time of sanction.
relatedSections: Section 24(b) — Interest on home loan (₹2,00,000 per year for self-occupied property; ₹80EE is over and above this)
Section 80EEA — Additional interest deduction for affordable housing loans (April 2019 – March 2022)
Section 80C — Principal repayment of home loan
Section 115BAC — New tax regime (80EE not available)

workedExample.profile: First-time buyer. Loan of ₹32 lakh sanctioned October 2016 for a ₹45 lakh flat. Annual interest in FY 2025-26: ₹2,30,000. Section 24(b): ₹2,00,000. Section 80EE: ₹30,000 (remaining interest). Total interest deduction: ₹2,30,000.
workedExample.taxSaving: 80EE additional deduction ₹30,000 (capped at ₹50,000 limit). At 30% slab: ₹9,000 + 4% cess = ₹9,360 additional tax saved beyond Section 24(b) benefit.
